config.resprouted.title: Resprouted Configuration
config.resprouted.oiled_saturation_modif: Multiplier for saturation when eating oiled foods
config.resprouted.oiled_hunger_bonus.too: Extra hunger points when eating oiled foods
config.resprouted.enable_honey_bottle_ef: When enabled, honey bottles gives Regeneration Effect
config.resprouted.enable_vanta_oiling.to: When enabled, you can access to vanta oil crafting.
config.resprouted.vanta_oil_whitelist.to: List of item IDs that can be craftable with vanta oil and elixirs
config.resprouted.vanta_oil_blacklist.to: List of vanilla item IDs that can't be craftable with vanta oil and elixirs
config.resprouted.enable_olive_oiling.to: When enabled, you have the ability to add olive oil to food.
config.resprouted.oilable_food_whitelist: List of item IDs that can be olive oiled. One ID per line.
config.resprouted.oilable_food_blacklist: List of vanilla and Resprouted mod item IDs that can't be olive oiled. One ID per line.
When enabled, custom bottles will show their effect information in the tooltip.
config.resprouted.food_effect_tooltip.to: When enabled, custom food will show their effect information in the tooltip.
config.resprouted.max_brew_time.tooltip: The total time, in ticks, required for a brewing process to complete. | Range: 1200 (1 min) - 120000 (100 min). | Default: 12000 (10 min).
rei.resprouted.brewing_barrel_tooltip: The starter culture fluid is optional
text.resprouted.main: Here you will find everything related to the mod. This almanac contains guides, and useful information about all the added features.
text.resprouted.fertile_soil: Fertile soil works like regular farmland but it doesn’t need water to stay hydrated, can’t be trampled and lets sugar cane grow without a nearby water block
text.resprouted.crop_stakes.1: Crop stakes are wooden poles that support the growth of tall crops such as chili peppers and tomatoes. They can also be used to tie ropes.
text.resprouted.tomatoes.1: Tomatoes grow on crop stakes placed over fertile soil or farmland. You can find seeds by breaking tall and normal grass and plant them by interacting with a stake while holding the seeds.
text.resprouted.tomatoes.2: The vines can reach up three blocks in height. To harvest, interact with the plant instead of breaking it. Tomatoes can be eaten or even thrown at others while sneaking.
text.resprouted.chili_peppers.1: Chili peppers grow on crop stakes placed over fertile soil or farmland. You can find seeds by breaking tall and normal grass and plant them by interacting with a stake while holding the seeds.
text.resprouted.chili_peppers.2: Mature plants produce chili peppers and can reach up to two blocks in height. To harvest, interact with the plant instead of breaking it. Chili peppers can be eaten for a short speed boost, though their spiciness might burn you. They also work as an alchemy ingredient.
text.resprouted.chili_peppers.3: Sometimes, harvesting may yield a ghost pepper. Eating one gives you temporary fire powers, allowing you to shoot fireballs.
text.resprouted.grapes.1: Grapes come from vines that grown from seeds planted in fertile soil or farmland. These seeds are found by breaking vines. With ropes above, the stems the stems will climb and form grape vines.
text.resprouted.grapes.2: Grapes can be collected by interacting with the vine. Breaking it slows its recovery, so it's better to leave it intact. Grapes can be eaten or turned into juice with a crushing tub, which can be bottled and brewed into wine.
text.resprouted.apple_tree.1: Apple trees grow from saplings obtained by crushing apples for seeds. They yield oak wood but are more reliable for apple production than regular oaks.
text.resprouted.apple_tree.2: Their leaves generate apples when exposed to air, and ripe apples can be collected by interacting with the leaves. Breaking leaves isn’t ideal, since they don’t regrow, though they can drop saplings and any apples inside.
text.resprouted.apple_tree.3: Crushing apples also produces seeds and juice, which can be bottled or brewed into cider.
text.resprouted.ironwood_trees.desc1: Ironwood Trees grow naturally in the overworld in forests, plains, mountains, swamps, and jungles. They do not grow naturally in dry biomes.
text.resprouted.ironwood_trees.desc2: The logs can be crafted into ironwood planks, which can be used to make ironwood variants of many wood blocks. Ironwood leaves drop ironwood saplings and iron berries.
text.resprouted.olives.desc1: Olives grow on olive trees found naturally in forests, plains and mountains, but never in snowy areas. Their logs can be crafted into olive wood planks, which serve to create olive wood versions of many wooden blocks. The leaves drop saplings and olives.
text.resprouted.olives.desc2: Raw olives should not be eaten, as they contain harmful toxins. When crushed in a crushing tub, they produce olive oil. This oil is too strong to enjoy on its own, but when combined with food in a crafting table, it makes meals more filling and satisfying. Olive oil can be added to any food.
text.resprouted.herbs.desc1: Herbs appear naturally across many climates in both the overworld and the nether. Some can be eaten, and all of them serve as ingredients or modifiers in alchemy. Once collected, herbs can be planted as crops.
text.resprouted.herbs.desc2:
Aloe vera: spawns in sandy biomes, savanna biomes, and mesa biomes. 

Chamomile: spawns in plains biomes, forest biomes and swamp biomes.

Cloudsbluff: spawns in mountain biomes. It is edible and gives the levitation effect on consumption.
text.resprouted.herbs.desc3:
Cohosh: spawns in forest biomes. 

Core root: spawns in caves and is edible. 

Deathstalk mushrooms: spawn in the nether. 

Ginseng: spawns in plains and forest biomes. It is edible.
text.resprouted.herbs.desc4:
Horsetail: spawns in jungle, swamp, plains and forest biomes. 

Marsh mallow: spawns in jungle and swamp biomes. It is edible. 

Mooncap mushroom: spawns in jungle biomes and caves. 

Wind thistle: spawns in mountain and plains biomes.
text.resprouted.herbs.desc5: Vanta lily: spawns in plains and forest biomes. It can be crushed into vanta oil.
text.resprouted.liquid_barrel.desc: Liquid barrels can store up to 16 buckets of liquid and keep their contents even when broken. They only accept liquids that are not gaseous and remain below a certain temperature. If left in the rain, they will gradually fill with water.
text.resprouted.cabinets.desc1: Cabinets are a decorative storage alternative to chests. They can be oriented or mirrored for flexible placement. If you place one cabinet on top of another with the same orientation, they will combine into a double cabinet.
text.resprouted.cabinets.desc2: A double cabinet has the same storage capacity as a large chest. Cabinets can only be opened from their front side.
text.resprouted.rope.desc1: Rope serves as a ladder replacement, decoration, and grapevine support. It attaches horizontally to solid blocks or hangs vertically from solid blocks or other ropes.
text.resprouted.rope.desc2: To extend a rope vertically, use a rope on an existing rope block to place a new segment below it. Dispensers with rope can automate this extension when aimed at a rope block. Ropes can also be tied to crop stakes, which then function as anchor points. Shooting a rope with an arrow breaks it.
text.resprouted.chains.desc: Chains are a scalable alternative to the vanilla ones. They can be extended vertically by using a chain on an existing one to place another below it. They are available in gold and copper variants.
text.resprouted.candle_holder.desc1: Candle Holders are decorative light sources that can be placed on any side of solid blocks, similar to torches. They are crafted with a candle, a nugget, and an ingot of either iron, gold, or copper.
text.resprouted.candle_holder.desc2: The copper variant possesses all the properties of a vanilla copper block, including oxidation. They look especially nice when placed on the sides of a chandelier ring.
text.resprouted.chandeliers.desc1: Chandeliers are decorative blocks that can hold torches and candles on their sides. They must be suspended from a rope, chain, or solid block above them to avoid falling.
text.resprouted.chandeliers.desc2: A falling chandelier deals massive damage to any entity beneath it. They are available in copper, iron, and gold variants.
text.resprouted.table.desc: Tables are decorative blocks. The type of planks used in their crafting determines their appearance. When placed next to each other, tables connect seamlessly, with legs only appearing at the corners of the combined structure.
text.resprouted.chair.desc: Chairs are decorative blocks whose appearance is determined by the planks used to craft them. Right-click on a chair to sit in it, and sneak to get up.
text.resprouted.stool.desc: Stools are decorative blocks whose appearance is determined by the planks used to craft them. Right-click on a stool to sit in it, and sneak to get up.
text.resprouted.gargoyle.desc: Gargoyles are decorative statues of monstrous creatures that can be oriented to face any of the four cardinal directions.
text.resprouted.slate.desc: Slate is a stone type found throughout the world. It can be crafted into various decorative blocks but is primarily used as a roofing material.
text.resprouted.pillar.desc: Stone Pillars are decorative blocks that can be crafted from various stone types and oriented along any of the three axes.
text.resprouted.clay_walls.desc: Clay Walls are an attractive building material available in three variants, each featuring a different support beam pattern.
text.resprouted.painted_planks.desc: Painted Planks come in sixteen different colors to add more visual variety to your builds. They are crafted by surrounding a dye with any type of wood planks, yielding the painted planks in the dye's color.
text.resprouted.crushing.desc1: A crushing tub turns solid items into fluids. It can hold up to one stack of items and eight buckets of liquid. To use it, place the tub and insert items by interacting with it while holding them. You can remove items with an empty hand.
text.resprouted.crushing.desc2: If the inserted items match a valid recipe, jumping on the tub will crush them into fluid, one at a time. Crushing won’t work if the tub is too full. You can empty the fluid by interacting with an empty bucket or a glass bottle. Some recipes also produce item byproducts.
text.resprouted.drying.desc1: A drying basin extracts items from fluids through evaporation. It can hold up to one stack of items and three buckets of liquid. To use it, place the basin and insert fluid by interacting with it while holding a filled bucket.
text.resprouted.drying.desc2: You can remove fluid with an empty bucket or a glass bottle. Evaporation happens automatically over time, at a rate of one fiftieth of a bucket per second. When enough fluid has evaporated to match a recipe, the result will appear in the basin if there is space.
text.resprouted.drying.desc3: Items can be collected by interacting with the basin with an empty hand.
text.resprouted.condenser.desc1: Alchemy is the process of brewing elixirs using condensers. To work properly, condensers need retorts attached to them. Making an elixir requires water, a glass bottle, ingredients and optionally modifiers, along with fuel.
text.resprouted.condenser.desc2: Basic condensers can brew elixirs with up to two ingredients. They must have two retorts connected, one on each side, facing the condenser so their tubes link correctly. Condensers have an internal tank that can be filled or emptied with a bucket. Sneaking and interacting with an empty hand will clear the tank completely.
text.resprouted.condenser.desc3: The condenser’s GUI shows the fluid inside. The two slots on the left are for ingredients, and their arrangement doesn’t matter. The top-right slot is for glass bottles, and the large slot below it is the output. The bottom-middle slot is for fuel.
text.resprouted.advanced_condenser.desc1: Advanced condensers work much like basic ones, but with a few important differences. They require three advanced retorts, attached to the lower half on the left, right and back sides, relative to the direction the condenser is facing.
text.resprouted.advanced_condenser.desc2: The retorts must be connected properly, with their tubes facing the condenser. These condensers can brew elixirs with up to three ingredients, plus an optional modifier. Modifiers adjust certain properties of the final elixir and can be placed in the top-middle slot of the GUI, just above the fuel slot.
text.resprouted.advanced_condenser.desc3: The interface also includes a third ingredient slot, in addition to the two found in basic condensers. Advanced condensers can brew any elixir that a basic condenser can.
text.resprouted.elixirs.desc1: Elixirs are magical mixtures created through alchemy using condensers. They behave much like potions but can be stacked up to sixteen. Unlike potions, they cannot be combined with arrows or turned into splash or lingering forms.
text.resprouted.vanta_oil.desc1: Vanta oil is made by crushing vanta lilies. It can be combined with most melee weapons and any number of identical elixirs or potions to give the weapon their effects. When striking a creature, the weapon uses some of its oil to apply the effect to the target.
text.resprouted.vanta_oil.desc2: Non-instant effects last around fifteen seconds per hit, and repeated strikes extend the duration. You can recharge an oiled weapon by crafting it again with more vanta oil and more elixirs or potions of the same type.
text.resprouted.alcohol.desc1: Alcoholic drinks, or booze, are made by brewing certain fluids in a brewing barrel. These drinks can grant powerful effects when used wisely, but may also cause trouble. Every booze has a quality between 0 and 1.
text.resprouted.alcohol.desc2: If the quality is at least 0.5, the effect will be positive. If it’s lower, the effect will be negative. The strength and duration of the effect depend on how close the quality is to either extreme. Booze with low quality is best discarded. You can do this easily by placing the bottle in a crafting table to get an empty glass bottle.
text.resprouted.alcohol.desc3: No matter the quality, drinking booze may cause inebriation. This is a debuff that cannot be cured with milk. Drinking more while inebriated increases its severity.  Levels I and II have no effect, but level III causes slowness I and blindness I.
text.resprouted.alcohol.desc4:
Level IV adds slowness II, blindness I and nausea II. 

Ale: Is a very saturating drink, and it applies the Full Stomach effect, which decreases damage taken from starvation.
text.resprouted.alcohol.desc5:
Ale wort: Ale is brewed with ale wort, which is made by combining a water bucket, bread, and sugar in a crafting table.

Cider: This drink applies magic resistance effect, which reduces damage taken from magical sources.
text.resprouted.alcohol.desc6:
Iron wine: Grants the drinker absorption hearts with no time limit, up to ten extra hearts.

Mead: Applies the wither ward effect, which reduces damage taken from the wither effect.
text.resprouted.alcohol.desc7:
Sweet Berry Wine: Increases the level of all positive effects, up to level III.

Wine: Increases the duration of all positive effects by up to ten minutes.
text.resprouted.alcohol.desc8: Ambrosia: Protects the drinker from death, much like a totem of undying, though only for a limited time. The strongest ambrosia, however, lasts until death occurs. Unlike other alcoholic brews, ambrosia rarely has low quality, and higher-quality batches are generally easier to produce.
text.resprouted.brewing.desc1: Brewing is the process of fermenting fluids into alcohol using a Brewing Barrel. The system uses three fluid tanks: one for the input fluid, one for the output, and an optional one for a starter culture. The starter culture must match the type of the output fluid.
text.resprouted.brewing.desc2: Containers like buckets or bottles can be placed in the slots above each tank to fill or empty them automatically. The brewing process lasts half a day (10 minutes) and begins automatically when the input tank contains fluid and the starter culture (if used) is valid.
text.resprouted.brewing.desc3: Brewing without a starter culture produces alcohol with a random quality between 0.05 and 0.75. To improve quality, use your best batch as a starter culture for the next brew.
text.resprouted.brewing.desc4: Repeat this process, replacing the starter culture with higher-quality output each time, until you achieve the desired quality. The starter culture fluid is not consumed during brewing.
